items.title: Walter Sinnott-Armstrong - Do Angels and Demons Exist?
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>Sign up for a free Closer To Truth membership to receive exclusives like early access to new episodes: https://closertotruth.com/register/

Many theologians take angels and demons seriously. Why? Certainly, most human beings believe in angels and demons. Anecdotal cases are legion. Certainly, such nonphysical beings, in one form or another, populate most of the world’s religions. But angels and demons seem out of place, anachronistic, in our modern world. Is there anything here we should take seriously?

Make a tax-deductible donation of any amount to help support Closer To Truth continue making content like this: https://shorturl.at/OnyRq

Alexander Rosenberg is an American philosopher and novelist. He is the R. Taylor Cole Professor of Philosophy at Duke University, well known for contributions to philosophy of biology and philosophy of economics. Rosenberg describes himself as a "naturalist".

items.title: Overcoming Challenges in AI Agent Deployment: The Sweet Spot for Governance and Security // Spencer Reagan // #349
items.author: Demetrios
items.content:
leads R&D at Airia, working on secure AI-agent orchestration, data governance systems, and real-time signal fusion technologies for regulated and defense environments.

Overcoming Challenges in AI Agent Deployment: The Sweet Spot for Governance and Security // MLOps Podcast #349 with Spencer Reagan, R&D at Airia.

Shoutout to Airia for powering this MLOps Podcast episode.

Spencer Reagan thinks it might be, and he’s not shy about saying so. In this episode, he and Demetrios Brinkmann get real about the messy, over-engineered state of agent systems, why LLMs still struggle in the wild, and how enterprises keep tripping over their own data chaos. They unpack red-teaming, security headaches, and the uncomfortable truth that most “AI platforms” still don’t scale. If you want a sharp, no-fluff take on where agents are actually headed, this one’s worth a listen.

Passionate about technology, software, and building products that improve people's lives.

items.title: Bernardo Kastrup - Idealism: Arguing Pro and Con?
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>Like us on Facebook for daily videos, updates, announcements, and much more: https://shorturl.at/tak4l

Idealism is the theory that all reality is consciousness and only consciousness, and that what we perceive to be the physical world is either a manifestation of consciousness or derived from it. Either way, fundamental reality is pure consciousness. What are the arguments for and against Idealism (like the common sense of the physical)?

Donate to help Closer To Truth continue exploring the world's deepest questions without the need for paywalls: https://closertotruth.com/donate/

Bernardo Kastrup is a Dutch philosopher and computer engineer whose work centers on consciousness studies and analytic idealism, a form of metaphysical idealism developed within the analytic tradition. He questions physicalism and argues that consciousness lies at the foundation of reality.

Wear your support for the show with a Closer To Truth merchandise purchase: https://bit.ly/3P2ogje

items.title: Panel: Productivity gains through AI?!- S.Richter, D.Proppe, F.Wesner, T.Schlottke | CTO Summit 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content:
<article>The research is unclear, and our teams’ personal experiences with implementing agentic systems are mixed. While we enjoyed success in small projects—and half of LinkedIn seems to claim they’ve found the holy grail of agentic coding or how to use LLMs for productivity gains—the truth is less clear-cut.

This panel will explore why these experiences are so mixed and what could actually help our teams work better with AI in the background.</article>

items.title: What CTOs Can Learn from World Record Freediving - Tom Sietas | CTO Summit 2025 Hamburg
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content: <article>Tom Sietas, 22-time freediving world record holder and author of „Deine Atmung ändert alles!“, shares how targeted breathing techniques regulate stress and enhance performance under extreme pressure. In this talk, he bridges the gap between high-performance sports and tech leadership: how mastering your own physiology can improve decision-making, resilience, and focus in critical moments. Attendees will gain practical tools to apply simple breathing strategies for sharper thinking and sustainable energy in their role as technology leaders.</article>

items.title: When I met Craig he was 13 and homeless. I still thought his life might turn around. I was tragically wrong
Renaissance Beauty (Radio Edit)
items.content:
Greg Jenner is joined in 16th-century Italy by historian Professor Jill Burke and comedian Tatty Macleod to learn all about Renaissance beauty standards and treatments.

Early modern Italy is renowned for the gorgeous artworks created by painters like Titian, Rubens and Botticelli, many of them featuring beautiful women looking at themselves in mirrors or getting made up for a night out. In this episode, we take you through a Renaissance Get Ready With Me as we explore how these women would have been taking care of their hair and skin. We look at what hairstyles and makeup men and women wore, how often they bathed, whether or not they removed their body hair, and how they shaped their bodies through dieting and underwear. Along the way, we dive into the recipes for popular cosmetics and skincare treatments, ask where Renaissance beauty standards came from, and uncover the sexist, racist and classist ideas that often underpinned them. But we also explore how their beauty routines could be an avenue for women’s self-expression, and show the importance of the history of beauty, even amidst the turbulent politics and warfare of the early modern period.

This is a radio edit of the original podcast episode. For the full-length version, please look further back in the feed.

Hosted by: Greg Jenner Research by: Emma Bentley Written by: Emmie Rose Price-Goodfellow, Emma Nagouse, and Greg Jenner Produced by: Emmie Rose Price-Goodfellow and Greg Jenner Audio Producer: Steve Hankey Production Coordinator: Gill Huggett Senior Producer: Emma Nagouse Executive Editor: Philip Sellars

items.title: Pydantic AI with Samuel Colvin
items.author: SEDaily
items.content:
Python’s popularity in data science and backend engineering has made it the default language for building AI infrastructure. However, with the rapid growth of AI applications, developers are increasingly looking for tools that combine Python’s flexibility with the rigor of production-ready systems.

Pydantic began as a library for type-safe data validation in Python and has become one of the language’s most widely adopted projects. More recently, the Pydantic team created Pydantic AI, a type-safe agent framework for building reliable AI systems in Python.

Samuel Colvin is the creator of Pydantic and Pydantic AI. In this episode, he joins the podcast with Gregor Vand to discuss the origins of Pydantic, the design principles behind type safety in AI applications, the evolution of Pydantic AI, the LogFire observability platform, and how open-source sustainability and engineering discipline are shaping the next generation of AI tooling.

items.title: Sitting Bull and the Battle of Little Bighorn
items.content:
The Battle of the Little Bighorn - also known as the Battle of the Greasy Grass - was one of the most dramatic and important clashes in American history. In June 1876, on the rolling plains of Montana, Colonel George Armstrong Custer and the 7th Cavalry charged into a vast encampment of Lakota and other tribes — and were utterly destroyed by the superior native forces who fought to defend their sacred lands from the encroaching United States.

In this episode, Dan is joined by former National Parks historian Paul Hedren to explore how this battle came to define the struggle between the U.S. government and the Plains tribes, what led to Custer’s fatal mistake, and how the Lakota, Cheyenne, and Arapaho achieved a stunning, if fleeting, victory.

Paul's new book is called 'Sitting Bull's War: The Battle of the Little Bighorn and the Fight for Buffalo and Freedom'.

Produced by Mariana Des Forges and edited by Dougal Patmore.

items.title: Origins of Chocolate
items.content:
Tristan Hughes is joined by Dr. Cameron McNeil, Mesoamerican archaeologist, to explore cacao, in ancient Mesoamerican societies like the Maya, Aztecs, and Olmecs. They discuss how was used as food, drink, currency, and in ritualistic practices, and learn about its journey from South America to becoming a highly valued commodity in Mesoamerica.

Presented by Tristan Hughes. The audio editor and producer is Joseph Knight. The senior producer is Anne-Marie Luff.

All music courtesy of Epidemic Sounds

The Ancients is a History Hit podcast.

You can take part in our listener survey

items.title: From the archive: A day in the life of (almost) every vending machine in the world
The Beatles: The Band that Changed the World, with Conan O’Brien (Part 1)
Hardening Agents for E-commerce Scale: From RL Alignment to Reliability // Panel 2
items.author: Demetrios
items.content:
Thanks to Prosus Group for collaborating on the Agents in Production Virtual Conference 2025.

The discussion centers on highly technical yet practical themes, such as the use of advanced post-training techniques like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) to ensure LLMs maintain stability while specializing for e-commerce domains. We compare the implementation challenges of Computer-Using Agents in automating legacy enterprise systems versus the stability issues faced by conversational agents when inputs become unpredictable in production. We will analyze the role of cloud infrastructure in supporting the continuous, iterative training loops required by Reinforcement Learning-based agents for e-commerce!

Paul van der Boor (Panel Host) //

Paul van der Boor is a Senior Director of Data Science at Prosus and a member of its internal AI group.

Arushi Jain (Panelist) //

Arushi is a Senior Applied Scientist at Microsoft, working on LLM post-training for Computer-Using Agent (CUA) through Reinforcement Learning. She previously completed Microsoft’s competitive 2-year AI Rotational Program (MAIDAP), building and shipping AI-powered features across four product teams.

She holds a Master’s in Machine Learning from the University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, and a Dual Degree in Economics from IIT Kanpur. At Michigan, she led the NLG efforts for the Alexa Prize Team, securing a $250K research grant to develop a personalized, active-listening socialbot. Her research spans collaborations with Rutgers School of Information, Virginia Tech’s Economics Department, and UCLA’s Center for Digital Behavior.

Beyond her technical work, Arushi is a passionate advocate for gender equity in AI. She leads the Women in Data Science (WiDS) Cambridge community, scaling participation in her ML workshops from 25 women in 2020 to 100+ in 2025—empowering women and non-binary technologists through education and mentorship.

Passionate about building and investing in cutting-edge technology to drive positive impact.

Currently shaping the future of AI/ML at Google Cloud.

10+ years of global experience across the U.S, EMEA, and India in product, strategy & venture capital (Google, Uber, BCG, Morpheus Ventures).

I’m passionate about making AI more useful and safe.

Why? Because AI will be ubiquitous in every workflow, powering our lives just like how electricity revolutionized our society - It’s pivotal we get it right.

At Inworld AI, we believe all future software will be powered by voice. As a Sr Product Manager at Inworld, I'm focused on building a real-time voice API that empowers developers to create engaging, human-like experiences. Inworld offers state-of-the-art voice AI at a radically accessible price - No. 1 on Hugging Face and Artificial Analysis, instant voice cloning, rich multilingual support, real-time streaming, and emotion plus non-verbal control, all for just $5 per million characters.

Experienced Product Leader with over 10 years in the tech industry, shaping impactful solutions across micro-mobility, e-commerce, and leading organizations in the new economy, such as OLX, iFood, and now Nubank. I began my journey as a Product Owner during the early days of modern product management, contributing to pivotal moments like scaling startups, mergers of major tech companies, and driving innovation in digital banking.

My passion lies in solving complex challenges through user-centered product strategies. I believe in creating products that serve as a bridge between user needs and business goals, fostering value and driving growth. At Nubank, I focus on redefining financial experiences and empowering users with accessible and innovative solutions.

items.title: Rust Beyond The Hype - Matthias Endler | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content: <article>It's strange how much the public opinion about Rust being a hipster language differs from the reality of normal teams in medium-sized, German (and international) companies ship Rust code to production every day. What's up with that? Let's explore some lesser known facts about one of the most misunderstood programming languages.</article>

items.title: Linking Backstage and GitHub Copilot - Erik Doernenburg | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content: <article>The main focus of coding assistants like Copilot is the generation of code, but they can help with other development activities, too. Based on work with a large enterprise, Erik will walk you through buillding a GitHub Copilot extension that brings information from the Backstage developer portal directly into Copilot as an agent. The extension is written in Python and showcases a full implementation of the retrival augmented generation (RAG) pattern. Knowledge of this pattern is useful in may different contexts as it has proven to be the sensible default for building information retrival systems based on LLM's. Erik will show semantic search, prompt rewriting, reranking and use of the ragas library for evals in very practical terms while implementing the extension.</article>

items.title: AI is not Software - Stephan Schmidt | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content: <article>In this thought-provoking talk, I challenge the widespread misconception that AI is merely another form of software development. For developers who have mastered traditional programming paradigms, AI represents a fundamentally different approach to problem-solving. Throughout human history, we've witnessed evolutionary leaps from mechanical to electrical to electronic tools, from software to hardware, with software representing just one phase in this progression. AI marks the next transformative step in this journey. Developers who continue treating AI as just another library or framework risk becoming obsolete, much like assembly programmers who couldn't adapt to high-level languages. Understanding AI as a distinct paradigm requiring new mental models, skills, and development practices is crucial for developers looking to remain relevant and innovative in the technological landscape of tomorrow.</article>

items.title: Solo-founding an AI startup with AI - Christoph Hartmann | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content: <article>Everybody is vibe-coding but how much more does it take to go from a vibe project to a SaaS app with customers? How much can we do with AI and where are the limits? Do you still need a cofounder? Do you still need to raise money? I started solo-founding a SaaS workplace productivity app (donethat.ai) two month ago and launched a few weeks ago. By the time of this talk we'll know what worked and what didn't and I'll share everything I learnt on the way.</article>

items.title: How to scrape modern websites to feed AI agents - Tobias Potocek | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content:
<article>It was web scraping that quietly enabled the AI revolution.

All major generative AI models and large language models (LLMs) have been trained on massive datasets extracted from the web. But the story doesn’t end there. Today’s AI agents still rely on up-to-date information from online sources to stay relevant, reduce hallucinations, and go beyond their training data. Once again, the web becomes the richest source of context.

Yet scraping the modern web is far from trivial. From anti-bot protections to dynamic, client-side rendering and long-term maintenance issues, building scalable data pipelines has become increasingly complex.

Whether you're building copilots, smart assistants, or knowledge-augmented LLM apps, this talk will equip you with the techniques and tools to make the web your AI's best friend.</article>

items.title: Building Agents with the AI SDK - Josh Singh | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content: <article>Agents are the next step in how we build with AI. In this talk I’ll share how to go from a simple prompt to a reliable multi-step agent using the AI SDK. The AI SDK is an open-source framework from Vercel that gives developers a unified way to work with models, connect tools, and handle streaming, memory, and reasoning. I’ll show how to use it to build agents that call APIs, write code, and make decisions through a clear step-based architecture. You’ll see how it can fit into your existing stack and what types of apps you can create with it. By the end, you’ll know how to start building your own agents and how to think about them as real software systems.</article>

items.title: The Knowledge Gap: Why AI Agents Fail in Real Life - Matthias Lau | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content:
<article>AI agents look great on stage but stumble in real life. They tell you the outdated launch date, suggest writing a follow-up that's already been sent, and keep replying with long texts after you asked for bullet points. What’s missing is the background knowledge humans carry without thinking — memories, experience, preferences.

This talk follows Dev Dana, an engineer whose shiny demo agent falls apart the moment it meets real-world workflows. Step by step, you’ll see how Dana:

- adds long-term memory to a chat-only agent, so context from earlier conversations stays available
- uncovers RAG’s role — and its limits
- connects relationship-aware graphs and external data feeds — calendars, docs, email — to keep the agent informed

By the end, Dana’s once-clueless agent navigates projects like a pro. Come for the story, leave with insights you can drop into your own stack to close the knowledge gap for good.</article>

items.title: Unleashing the Power of Knowledge Graphs for Next-Gen RAG (GraphRAG) - F. Krause | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content: <article>Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a powerful technique for leveraging large language models (LLMs). However, traditional RAG, relying on vector embeddings, often struggles with complex reasoning and factual consistency. This presentation introduces GraphRAG, a novel approach that enhances RAG pipelines using knowledge graphs – a technology that has been around for years. What's new is that LLMs can now assist in creating and maintaining these knowledge graphs. By encoding domain knowledge into a structured graph, we can achieve more precise, context-aware retrieval, leading to more accurate and trustworthy LLM responses. We'll explore the architecture of GraphRAG, highlighting how LLMs can be used for knowledge graph construction and maintenance, and demonstrate how it overcomes the limitations of traditional vector-based RAG. Attendees will gain a clear understanding of the benefits of GraphRAG and learn how to leverage knowledge graphs, now more accessible than ever</article>

items.title: AI: The Double-Edged Sword in the Fight Against Disinformation - Tereza Iofciu | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content:
<article>AI is everywhere, helping us spot fake news, but also making it easier to create. Ever wondered just how much AI is shaping the information you see, or  what to do if you plan to use AI to fight back against disinformation?

In this talk, I'll break down the role of AI in the space of creating mis and disinformation, and what you can really do to build smarter, safer systems. We’ll look at real-world examples, surprising pitfalls, and practical ways to combine AI with human expertise.</article>

items.title: GitOps: Automatisiert, sicher & wartungsfrei mit ArgoCD, Kured & Renovate - Castro | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content: <article>In modernen Kubernetes-Setups wird "Automatisierung" oft versprochen – aber was heißt das konkret im Alltag? In diesem Talk zeige ich, wie GitOps mit Tools wie ArgoCD, Kured und Renovate nicht nur den Code, sondern auch Updates und Sicherheitsmaßnahmen automatisiert. Dabei geht es nicht nur um Theorie, sondern um echte Use-Cases, Live-Demos und praktische Tipps, wie man Infrastrukturen wartungsfreier und sicherer macht.</article>

items.title: GitHub Coding Agents in Action: Solving Real-World DevOps Challenges | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content:
<article>Discover how GitHub Coding Agents help to automate everyday tasks like refactoring and enables self-healing systems to tackle complex DevOps scenarios.

See live demos of code transformation and failure recovery scenarios, plus best practices.</article>

items.title: Virtual Actors: Object-Oriented Programming That Automatically Scales- Heiner Wolf | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content:
<article>Tired of juggling caching strategies, database connections, and other distributed system complexities in your large scale app? Use Virtual Actors - a programming paradigm that extends traditional object-oriented principles to seamlessly operate across distributed environments. Virtual Actors provide a programming model which transparently handles Scaling, Persistence, Caching, Communication, Redundancy.

I'll demonstrate how Virtual Actors allow developers to focus on business logic while the framework automatically manages the complexities of distributed systems. This talk bridges theory and practice, showing concrete patterns that replace the traditional web server + database + cache architecture with a more elegant solution that allows you to focus on your business code rather than the infrastructure.

It's an open source project. It's what you would do and what you already have done whenever you needed it. Consolidated into a production ready project with a large community.</article>

items.title: Marine Corps-inspired leadership and planning for software teams - Andre Alves | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content:
<article>On the surface, planning and leadership for warfighting may seem worlds apart from the realities of software development. Yet many of the principles that make Marine Corps units effective under pressure, such as clarity of mission, adaptability, and empowered leadership, translate directly to engineering teams.

Drawing on my past experience as a U.S. Marine Corps officer and my current work as a software engineering team lead @freiheit.com, I’ll share how elements of the Marine Corps leadership philosophy and the Marine Corps Planning Process can improve the way we plan, lead, and deliver in tech. We’ll explore practical ideas: putting culture before process, defining clear end states, orienting teams with shared context, and setting priorities that align everyone’s effort. You’ll see how techniques used to coordinate complex military operations can be adapted for sprint planning, technical roadmaps, and cross-team collaboration while keeping people at the center of every decision.</article>

items.title: How we almost delivered 100 tons of Stracciatella Mousse - Wendland, Schroeder | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content:
<article>It's not a bug, it's a feature!

How often do we read or hear this saying? 

At what point in time are you confident to say that it's a feature?

Is it when your service has a very good test-coverage, a user-focused design and never acts out of line because it utilizes fancy front-end testing tools and canary-releases? 

Even with seemingly perfect coverage along all levels of the test pyramid, we wonder why bugs appear.

This talk is about how we almost delivered 100 tons of Stracciatella Mousse. On time!

This was not a bug in production. A store employee confirmed that specific order. 

Nevertheless this behaviour sounds strange enough to investigate.

That's why we stepped back. Took a deep breath and had a look at our metrics.

Can we actually detect anomalies before someone drowns in a pool of tasty Stracciatella Mousse. Join the talk to find out!</article>

items.title: How AI Agents Are Redefining DAO Governance - Christian Ziegler | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content:
<article>Governance in DAOs suffers from four issues: fragmented decision-making, low turnout, slow cycles, and lack of decentralization.

Part I: Delegation Flaws.

We analyze why one-size-fits-all delegation erases individual views and concentrates power. We show how this centralization drives stakeholder disengagement.

Part II: Delegate-as-Service Implementation.

User Profiling. We deploy “dating-profile” questionnaires to capture each holder’s policy priorities, risk appetite, and voting style. We encode answers as structured preference vectors.

RAG Design. We ingest on-chain proposals, voting histories, forum discussions, and off-chain signals. We index these in a vector store and retrieve context for each agenda item. An LLM then generates concise voting recommendations or executes votes directly on-chain.

This modular design preserves every individual’s intent, translates preferences into executable code, restores decentralization, speeds up governance, and boosts trust.</article>

items.title: Story: The Bug He Couldn't Name - A 15-Year Fight Inside One Developer's Mind
items.content:
Imagine facing a problem you can't name, something that feels bigger than any bug you've ever had to fix. How do you debug your own mind when you don't even know what's wrong?

Burke Holland's story starts with a college party and a bad trip that leaves a deeper mark than he expects. Sleep gets harder. Fear creeps in. His life starts shrinking. School falls apart, friends drift away, and he ends up back at home trying to understand what's happening to him.

He looks for structure in the Coast Guard. Later he discovers computers and realizes he might have found the thing he's meant to do. But the shadow that followed him out of that party doesn't care about career paths. It shows up during college, during work, during marriage, during parenthood. Sometimes it's quiet, sometimes it knocks him completely flat.

This is the story of a developer who looks effortless on stage but spent years fighting something no one else could see, and what changed once he finally understood what he was up against.

What do you do when the hardest problem in your life isn't in your code, but in yourself?

items.title: SED News: Bezos Returns to Building, AI’s Reality Check, and Europe’s Cloud Ambitions
items.author: SEDaily
items.content:
SED News is a monthly podcast from Software Engineering Daily where hosts Gregor Vand and Sean Falconer unpack the biggest stories shaping software engineering, Silicon Valley, and the broader tech industry.

In this episode, they cover Jeff Bezos’s unexpected return to the CEO seat with  Project Prometheus, the growing debate over whether AI investments are sustainable, and the ecosystem forming around OpenAI. They also dig into the surge of Nordic startups, and what it signals about innovation hotspots outside the Bay Area.

Gregor and Sean then dive deep into the idea of technology tipping points, when breakthrough products finally cross the threshold from fringe curiosity to mainstream adoption.

Finally, they highlight standout threads from Hacker News, including moss surviving in outer space, a typographer’s re-creation of San Francisco’s light-rail signage, CERN’s guiding principles for responsible AI adoption, and why classic Pixar films looked better on 35mm than they do in 4K.

items.title: What actually makes you senior (News)
items.content:
Matheus Lima on what makes senior developers actually senior, Tega Brain created a browser extension for avoiding AI slop, Andrew Kelley moves Zig from GitHub to Codeberg, Matias Heikkilä says there’s no free lunch for vibe coding, and your SSD data at rest might be at risk.

Changelog++ members save 1 minute on this episode because they made the ads disappear. Join today!

  • Namespace – Speed up your development and testing workflows using your existing tools. (Much) faster GitHub actions, Docker builds, and more. At an unbeatable price.

items.title: Roger Penrose on Mind & Consciousness | Closer To Truth Chats
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>This Giving Tuesday, make a tax-deductible donation to Closer To Truth and support our mission of exploring life's biggest questions: https://closertotruth.com/donate/

Throughout his career, Penrose has challenged conventional wisdom in physics, mathematics, and the philosophy of science. He has tackled some of the most profound questions of the universe: How do quantum mechanics and cosmology shape our understanding of reality? Can human consciousness be fully explained by physical laws, or does it transcend computation? This six-part interview series spans Penrose’s most influential ideas, from black holes and the Big Bang to Gödel’s incompleteness theorems, quantum gravity, and the role of mathematics in uncovering ultimate reality.

Shop for everyone on your list in the Closer To Truth merch store and support the show with your purchase: https://bit.ly/3P2ogje

Sir Roger Penrose is an English mathematician, mathematical physicist, philosopher of science, and Nobel Laureate in Physics. He is Emeritus Rouse Ball Professor of Mathematics at the University of Oxford, an emeritus fellow of Wadham College, Oxford, and an honorary fellow of St John's College, Cambridge, and University College London. Penrose has contributed significantly to the mathematical physics of general relativity and cosmology.

Watch more CTT Chats here: https://t.ly/jJI7e</article>

items.title: Àlex Gómez-Marín - Can Psi Affect Consciousness Theories?
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>Watch more videos on this topic here: https://shorturl.at/ZDfTE

If psi phenomena are real and without explanation in physics – telepathy, clairvoyance, precognition, psychokinesis – what would that suggest about the nature of consciousness, and which theories would be more consistent? We do not address whether or not psi phenomena are real, other than calling for critical thinking.

Sign up for a free Closer To Truth membership to receive exclusives like early access to new episodes: https://closertotruth.com/register/

Àlex Gómez-Marín is a Spanish physicist turned neuroscientist. His research spans from the origins of the arrow of time in inert systems, to the neurobiology of action-perception in living organisms such as flies, worms, mice, and humans. He is the head of the Behavior of Organisms Laboratory at the Instituto de Neurociencias in Alicante, where he is an Associate Professor of the Spanish Research Council.

Support the show with a tax-deductible donation of any amount:  https://shorturl.at/OnyRq

Closer To Truth, hosted by Robert Lawrence Kuhn and directed by Peter Getzels, presents the world’s greatest thinkers exploring humanity’s deepest questions. Discover fundamental issues of existence. Engage new and diverse ways of thinking. Appreciate intense debates. Share your own opinions. Seek your own answers.</article>

items.title: Tomorrow: Roger Penrose on mind and consciousness
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content: <article>Tomorrow at 11am ET, watch our interview with Nobel Laureate Roger Penrose on his radical ideas about mind and consciousness.</article>

items.title: ‘They take the money and go’: why not everyone is mourning the end of USAID
